questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.gold_answer: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, CIFAR-100
questions.system_answer: The evidence shows the paper pre-trained ViT on ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M. It also reports evaluation on ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.gold_answer: The largest DeiT variant is DeiT-B★ (DeiT-B with distillation) at 87M parameters.
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed is DeiT-B★, which has 87M parameters. The paper’s reference vision transformer is also described as 86M parameters, but the maximum among the provided variants is 87M.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.gold_answer: hierarchical structure, shifted window attention
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention within non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, and these design choices give linear computational complexity with respect to image size. The evidence also notes the use of relative position bias, while absolute position embedding is reported to hurt dense prediction tasks.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper presents DINO, a simple self-supervised Vision Transformer architecture based on self-distillation without labels: a student network is trained to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, using centering and sharpening to prevent collapse. It is designed to work with both ViTs and convnets, but the paper highlights that ViTs with small patches benefit especially strongly.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows a wide spread in reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy, from 1.3 up to 91.0 (spread 89.7), so the papers do not agree on a single value. There are also multiple conflicting SOTA claims, with different papers reporting top values such as 91.0 (CoCa finetuned), 90.9 (PaLI-17B), 90.6 (CoCa frozen), and 90.0 (MLP-Mixer), while other papers report lower best values like 88.7 and 86.5.

questions.question: What is the range of top-1 accuracy values reported on CIFAR-100 in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: The top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-100 in this corpus ranges from 48.5% to 91.88% across 27 results.
questions.system_answer: Across the corpus, reported CIFAR-100 top-1 accuracy values range from 48.5% to 91.88%, a spread of 43.38 percentage points. The highest value in the evidence is 91.88% (AdaptFormer-Swin), while the lowest is 48.5%; no explicit SOTA claims are listed in the provided evidence.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on which model holds SOTA on ADE20K mIoU?
questions.gold_answer: No—this corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model, since 7 papers each claim SOTA on the benchmark.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus does not fully agree: the highest ADE20K mIoU in the evidence is 62.9 for InternImage-H, but another paper, Vision Transformer Adapter for Dense Predictions, also makes a SOTA claim at 61.5 with ViT-Adapter-L. Several other papers report lower values (e.g., SwinV2-G at 59.9), so the SOTA claim is not consistent across papers, though InternImage-H is the top value shown here.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across papers, from 26.6 up to 62.9 (a spread of 36.3 points), so the corpus does not agree on a single value. There are also conflicting SOTA claims: InternImage-H is listed at 62.9, while ViT-Adapter-L is listed at 61.5 and SwinV2-G at 59.9, among others.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: No, the corpus does not agree. Some papers treat position embeddings as part of the standard ViT design, e.g. ViT says it “adds position embeddings,” while CPVT argues that learnable absolute positional encodings have problems and proposes conditional positional encodings instead. Other papers suggest they may be unnecessary or less important in some settings: CvT says “Removing position embedding of CvT does not degrade performance,” and the self-supervised ViT study says removing them “only slightly hurts performance.”

questions.question: How did the maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet change year over year in this corpus?
questions.system_answer: The maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet increased year over year from 88.08 in 2020 to 90.0 in 2021 and 91.0 in 2022, then declined to 87.8 in 2023 and 83.9 in 2024. So the trend was up from 2020–2022, then down from 2022–2024.
